one_line_takeaway: PoEtry recasts differentially private in-context learning as a Product-of-Experts over per-example log-probabilities (instead of hard-thresholded votes), giving a formal per-example DP guarantee plus large empirical accuracy gains (roughly 20-30 percentage points over prior DP-ICL baselines at small example counts) while cutting measured membership-inference AUROC close to chance.
key_finding: Soft (PoE) predictions substantially outperform hard-voting and subsampling baselines at matched privacy budgets, with the largest relative gains at small numbers of in-context examples (the practically relevant ICL regime). Concretely (Table 1, epsilon=4, Qwen3-4B): with 8 context examples, PoE reaches 86.3/87.3/78.9% accuracy on AGNews/DBPedia/TREC vs. RNM's 65.5/46.3/52.4% and PbS's 80.7/70.3/72.2%, and vs. Tang et al.'s synthetic-data method at 79.8/81.9/76.3% (which nominally protects a much larger training set, not the context set). At 25 examples the gap narrows but PoE still leads (87.0/88.0/78.8% vs. RNM 85.3/85.7/76.7% and PbS 81.5/75.2/68.1%). On GSM8k (Table 2), PoE reaches 37.3/40.9/43.1% at 4/8/20 examples vs. RNM's 15.7/20.3/36.5% (non-private ceiling 44.2/44.5/46.0%; 0-shot 14%). On the VLM pseudo-name task (Table 3, epsilon=1), PoE reaches 35.1/53.7/78.7% at 4/8/20 examples vs. RNM's 28.8/39.4/69.8% (non-private ceiling 89.0/89.1/89.8%; 0-shot 20%). Averaged across the four text/math datasets, the paper reports PoE beats RNM by about 30 percentage points at 4 examples, and RNM would need roughly 4x as many context examples (16 vs. 4) to match PoE's accuracy (Figure 2). A controlled ablation (Section 4.1, GSM8k, Qwen3, 3000 sampled examples) shows soft clipped predictions have higher mean predictive likelihood at the target label (45.3% vs 42.3% for hard) and lower worst-case (l-infinity norm) deviation from the true unclipped probability vector (13.5% vs 39.2%). The MIA results (Table 4) show the undefended (no-DP) ICL setting is empirically vulnerable to membership inference: AUROC of 60.0 (AGNews), 56.9 (DBpedia), 63.6 (TREC), and 93.9 (GSM8k); applying epsilon=1 DP reduces these to 52.9, 49.8, 53.8, and 53.5 respectively, close to the 50% chance baseline.
core_contribution: The paper reformulates differentially private in-context learning (DP-ICL) using a Product-of-Experts (PoE) model, named 'PoEtry'. Instead of conditioning an LLM on all in-context examples C1:J jointly, each example Cj is queried independently through the frozen LLM to obtain per-token log-probabilities; these log-probabilities are clipped (clip_gamma), summed across experts (equivalent to multiplying probabilities), and then sampled via the exponential mechanism to produce an (epsilon, delta)-DP output token. The key novelty over prior DP-ICL work is using the full 'soft' clipped log-probability vector from each expert rather than thresholded 'hard' one-hot votes (as in the Report-Noisy-Max / RNM baseline) or repeated subsampling (Privacy-by-Subsampling / PbS baseline), preserving predictive uncertainty that the authors argue is important for accuracy, especially with few in-context examples. The paper also proves (Theorem 3.1) that the algorithm satisfies (epsilon, delta)-DP under a per-example privacy unit, and (Theorem 3.4) a convergence result showing that, under a 'bounded cross-example interaction' assumption (Assumption 3.2), the PoE approximation converges almost surely to the full-context conditional distribution as the number of independent context 'views' J grows, at a stated rate of O(1/sqrt(J)) from the SLLN term plus O(1/J) from the vanishing residual.
threat_model: The threat model concerns privacy leakage from in-context examples supplied to a frozen LLM at inference time (not training-time leakage). The paper's own framing: an LLM/agent is given access to a small set of privacy-sensitive local examples (e.g., emails in an inbox, customer records, a robot's learned house layouts, client financial reports) via ICL or RAG/tool-calling, and an adversarial or merely curious query could cause the model's output to reveal information about those examples (citing Duan et al. 2023b, Choi et al., Wang et al. 2023 as prior evidence that ICL is vulnerable to such leakage). The privacy unit is a single in-context example: DP is defined with respect to two adjacent context sets differing by at most one example (per Chua et al. 2024a's user-level DP framing). The paper explicitly contrasts this per-context-set privacy guarantee with Tang et al. (2024)'s synthetic-data method, whose guarantee instead protects a much larger (100,000+ example) training dataset, not the actual context set used at inference. This is a genuine, paper-native security/privacy threat model, not one imposed by the reviewer.
limitations: The convergence theorem (Theorem 3.4) relies on Assumption 3.2 (bounded cross-example attention interaction, with the residual term bounded by a constant B independent of J) which the paper acknowledges is an approximation to actual transformer attention behavior and is not directly empirically verified against the true attention weights of the tested models -- it is justified qualitatively (limited cross-example attention, O(1/J) attention weights) rather than measured. The GSM8k evaluation converts a generative math task into a 10-way first-digit classification task, which the paper itself calls 'not standard.' The synthetic-data baseline (Tang et al. 2024) is evaluated with the authors' own re-implementation and a different (Qwen3-4B) model since the original trained models were unavailable, which the paper flags explicitly. The MIA evaluation uses a simplified likelihood-based proxy (summed log-likelihood as score) rather than a full LiRA attack with shadow models, and is run on only four of the five datasets (VLM excluded). The Impact Statement explicitly notes the method does not address disparate/fairness impacts of DP across demographic groups, citing this as open future work, and cautions that DP should not be treated as blanket justification for additional data access.

datasets_used: GSM8k (grade-school math, recast as 10-way first-digit classification)
Vision-language pseudo-name labelling task (5-way, image-name pairs, following Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021 setup)
quantitative_results: Table 1, epsilon=4, 8 context examples, Qwen3-4B: PoE 86.3/87.3/78.9% on AGNews/DBPedia/TREC vs RNM 65.5/46.3/52.4% vs PbS 80.7/70.3/72.2% vs synthetic-data 79.8/81.9/76.3% vs non-private ceiling 87.5/89.9/80.5%
Table 1, 25 context examples: PoE 87.0/88.0/78.8% vs RNM 85.3/85.7/76.7% vs PbS 81.5/75.2/68.1% vs non-private ceiling 87.8/92.0/81.0%
Table 2, GSM8k accuracy at 4/8/20 examples: PoE 37.3/40.9/43.1% vs RNM 15.7/20.3/36.5% vs non-private 44.2/44.5/46.0% (0-shot baseline 14%)
Table 3, VLM pseudo-name task at epsilon=1, 4/8/20 examples: PoE 35.1/53.7/78.7% vs RNM 28.8/39.4/69.8% vs non-private 89.0/89.1/89.8% (0-shot baseline 20%)
Section 4.1 ablation on GSM8k (3000 sampled examples, Qwen3): mean predictive likelihood at target label 45.3% (soft) vs 42.3% (hard); l-infinity norm deviation 13.5% (soft) vs 39.2% (hard)
Figure 2: average ~30 percentage point accuracy improvement of PoE over RNM at J=4 examples; RNM needs ~4x as many examples (16 vs 4) to match PoE accuracy
Table 4 MIA AUROC, no DP: 60.0 (AGNews), 56.9 (DBpedia), 63.6 (TREC), 93.9 (GSM8k); with epsilon=1 DP: 52.9, 49.8, 53.8, 53.5 respectively
baselines_compared: Report-Noisy-Max / RNM (hard one-hot voting + DP noise, Wu et al. 2024)
Privacy-by-Subsampling / PbS (Wu et al. 2024, repeated subsampled predictions)
Synthetic data generation (Tang et al. 2024, re-implemented by the authors on Qwen3-4B)
Non-private (no DP) in-context learning upper bound
